chunk_summary: Vegetarian diets in India require careful planning to address risks of vitamin B12, iron, calcium, and other deficiencies, especially in children. National data highlights high anemia prevalence and poor complementary feeding quality, necessitating tailored dietary counseling and monitoring.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical rationale for vegetarian dietary patterns in India, including nutrient risks.
Identify key micronutrient deficiencies associated with vegetarian and vegan diets in children.
Learn strategies for risk stratification, menu planning, and monitoring growth outcomes in vegetarian diets.
key_facts: Vegetarian diets in India include lacto-vegetarian, lacto-ovo vegetarian, and vegan patterns.
High-risk nutrients include vitamin B12, iron, calcium, vitamin D, zinc, and iodine, with risk increasing in vegan diets.
NFHS-5 reports high anemia prevalence in 6–59 month-old children and low complementary feeding quality in 6–23 month-olds.
Vitamin B12 and zinc deficiencies are documented in children and adolescents by CNNS data.
Well-planned vegetarian diets can support normal growth, but vegan diets require fortified foods or supplements to mitigate risks.
reference_quote: Well-planned vegetarian diets can support normal growth, but vegan diets show a steeper gradient of risk for vitamin B12 deficiency and for suboptimal bone indices when calcium/vitamin D intake is low. (Source: Position of the Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics: Vegetarian Diets, 2016)
document_key_topics: National burden of anemia and malnutrition
